facts_disclosed: Tried gas stove for 2 months, went back to charcoal — customers complained about taste difference

turns.content: have you ever try to make something faster and then it ruin everything?? because that is literally my life right now haha
Oh no, that sounds frustrating! What happened? Did you try to speed something up and it backfired?

okay so I sell khao man gai na ka. chicken rice. I been cooking on charcoal stove forever. my mom, my grandma, always charcoal. but charcoal is SLOW. you have to wait for it to heat up, fan it, add more during the day. so I think 'okay Pan you are smart girl, switch to gas stove, everything faster, serve more customers, make more money.' I buy a gas stove — 4,500 baht — and I use it for 2 months
I can see the logic — gas is faster, more consistent heat, easier to control. Two months is a decent trial too. So what went wrong? Did the speed boost actually help your sales?
at first yes!! gas heat up in 5 minutes instead of 30 minutes. no more fanning, no more charcoal dust on my hands, everything clean and modern. I was so happy na ka. I think I am genius 😂
Saving 25 minutes on heat-up time every morning is huge, especially with your early schedule. And less mess is always a plus. But from your opening message I'm guessing this genius plan didn't last?

no it did NOT last 😭 my regular customers start complaining. at first one or two people say 'Pan, the chicken taste different today.' I think maybe I put less ginger so I adjust. but then more people say it. and then one aunty — she eat my khao man gai EVERY DAY for 3 years — she come to me and say 'Pan, this is not your chicken anymore.' arai wa!! that sentence destroy me na ka

Oh wow — when a daily regular of three years says that, you know it's real. Her palate is basically calibrated to your exact flavour after hundreds of plates. That must have hit hard. Did you figure out right away that it was the stove causing the difference?

I go home that night and think about it. what is different?? same chicken, same rice, same sauce, same recipe. the ONLY thing I change is the stove. and then I understand — charcoal give a smoky flavour to the chicken skin na ka. it's subtle but it's THERE. gas burn clean so there is no smoke at all. my customers who eat every day, they can feel the difference even if they cannot explain exactly what is missing

That makes total sense. Charcoal gives off infrared heat differently from gas, and there's that subtle smokiness that infuses into the skin and fat. Gas burns completely clean — no smoke flavour. For something like khao man gai where the chicken skin is everything, your regulars would absolutely pick up on that missing dimension. Even if they can't say 'the charcoal smoke is gone,' they just know it's not the same.

YES exactly!! so I go back to charcoal. same day after that aunty tell me. I call my charcoal supplier that night and say 'bring me charcoal tomorrow morning please.' never again gas na ka. NEVER. I sell the gas stove to my friend Lek who sell pad thai — 3,000 baht — because pad thai don't need charcoal flavour. so I only lose 1,500 baht total

Same day — that's decisive! A lot of people would have tried to push through hoping customers would adjust, but you heard the feedback and acted immediately. And only losing 1,500 baht net after selling to Lek is basically a cheap tuition fee for a really important lesson. Did the aunty notice when it went back to charcoal?

she come the next day and she just nod at me. didn't say anything, just nod and smile. she KNOW haha. slowly all the other customers stop commenting too. everybody happy again. my mom always say 'if the old way work, don't change it just because you are lazy.' she was right na ka. some things you cannot rush. the charcoal is harder but the taste is the taste. that is what make my khao man gai special 🙏

The silent nod from the aunty — that's the highest compliment a daily regular can give. She didn't need words. And your mom's advice is perfect. You tried it, you learned why charcoal matters, and now you'll never wonder 'what if' again. The charcoal isn't just tradition for tradition's sake — it's genuinely part of the flavour. Three generations knew what they were doing. Go fan those coals with pride!
